summary: Gruber dissects a Wall Street Journal report claiming the next iPhone will replace Lightning with USB-C, finding the report poorly written and likely inaccurate. He notes the article's ambiguity about which models would switch and points out that Apple just invested heavily in Lightning accessories including AirPods and Lightning EarPods. He suggests the more plausible interpretation is that new iPhones will ship with USB-C chargers using a USB-C-to-Lightning cable, not that the phone's port itself will change. He also pushes back on the WSJ's framing that similar iPhone designs hurt sales, noting Apple just reported record quarterly iPhone sales.
key_points: The WSJ report is ambiguously written, failing to clarify which iPhone models would allegedly get USB-C or what 'no physical home button' means given iPhone 7 already lacks one
Apple's recent heavy investment in Lightning accessories (EarPods, AirPods case) makes a port switch illogical, and Gruber expects iPhones will keep Lightning until ports are eliminated entirely
The more likely interpretation is iPhones shipping with USB-C chargers connected via USB-C-to-Lightning cables, aligning with Apple's MacBook transition away from USB-A
The WSJ's narrative that similar iPhone designs cause sales slumps is contradicted by Apple's record first-quarter iPhone sales results
money_quotes: If Apple had any plans to switch from Lightning to USB-C, why wouldn't they have switched last year with the iPhone 7, when they started making tens of millions of pairs of Lightning ear buds?
My expectation has been that iPhones will never switch to USB-C — that Apple would stick with Lightning until they can do away with external ports entirely.
I have no inside dope on this, but it rings false to my ears.
If there's any truth to it, I'd bet that this year's iPhones will ship with USB-C chargers, that use a USB-C to Lightning cable to connect to the phones.
Apple just reported the highest iPhone sales in a quarter ever.
It's completely wrong to suggest that the similar-to-the-6/6S design of the iPhone 7 has led to a slump.
key_insight: The WSJ's USB-C iPhone rumor more likely refers to USB-C chargers with Lightning cables rather than a port change, and the article's framing of design-driven sales slumps is contradicted by Apple's own record results.
